paper_title: Simultaneous measurements of $N$-subjettiness observables in jets from gluons and light-flavour quarks, and in decays of boosted \texorpdfstring\PW

perturbations.why_wrong: The abstract claims a center-of-mass energy of 14 TeV, but the data description in Section 1 and the luminosity reference (CMS-PAS-LUM-17-003) explicitly state the data set was recorded at 13 TeV during 2016--2018.

Jet discriminants for N-prong decays are standardly defined as the ratio of the N-point shape to the (N-1)-point shape to quantify the resolution of subjets; reversing this to N+1 is inconsistent with the logic of identifying hadronic multi-prong decays described in the context.

The text references Ref. [1] (Datta and Larkoski) regarding the phase space of M emissions. For M particles, the phase space is 3M-4 dimensions after accounting for energy-momentum conservation and the fixed jet axis/invariant mass constraints; 3M-3 is a calculation error for this minimal basis.

The context describes splitting the ttbar sample into two mutually exclusive samples (W-enriched and top-enriched). If the selection criteria or channel labels are inconsistent with the lepton-plus-jets topology described later in the event selection, it indicates a failure in sample categorization.

The angular weighting exponent beta must be positive (typically beta > 0) for the N-subjettiness observable to be infrared and collinear safe and to properly measure radiation density; a negative value would cause the sum to diverge as Delta R approaches zero.

The normalization factor d_0 is defined to make the observable dimensionless and bounded. Changing the multiplication of the jet radius R^beta to division is mathematically inconsistent with the definition of N-subjettiness where R acts as the characteristic scale of the jet.

The N-subjettiness observable is defined as a measure of radiation 'about' N axes, which requires a minimization of the distance to the nearest axis. Changing 'min' to 'max' would measure the radiation furthest from the axes, contradicting the physical definition provided in the context.

The text states the basis is (3M-4)-dimensional for an M-body phase space. The perturbed indices (going up to M instead of M-1) would result in a different dimensionality, making the basis inconsistent with the (3M-4) constraint mentioned in the preceding sentence.

The text explicitly states the basis is shown for the '6-body phase space'. According to the (3M-4) logic established in the paper for M-body systems, the indices should correspond to the appropriate M-body limit. Changing the max index from 5 to 4 makes it inconsistent with the 6-body description.

The context defines 'b' as contributions from background processes. In a standard unfolding or signal extraction, backgrounds must be subtracted from the total observed data 'y' or added to the signal prediction 'Ax'. Changing the sign of 'b' relative to 'Ax' incorrectly treats background as a signal component or a negative contribution.

The weighted mean is defined using the sum of weights in the denominator. Dividing the covariance by the sum of squared weights instead of the sum of weights is mathematically inconsistent with the definition of a weighted average provided in the context.
